episode_title: Natali Helberger: Audience-Centric Approaches to AI Ethics in Media

theme_codings.theme_name: The Automation Paradox: Human-AI Labor Division in the Newsroom

best_quote.text: Someone who's a party here that is at the moment completely excluded from the loop are, for example, users. And their ability to feedback or to criticize or to let the media organization know when something goes wrong. And in our research we actually saw that users would very much appreciate being included in this loop.
best_quote.speaker: Natali Helberger

theme_codings.summary: The episode discusses transcription and translation as routine AI tools not requiring transparency disclosure, and extensively examines human-in-the-loop models, arguing users should be included in oversight loops alongside editorial staff.

theme_codings.theme_name: Trust on the Line: Credibility, Transparency, and the Audience Contract

best_quote.text: Being transparent about what parameter you use is very nice and good, but it starts only being really meaningful if you also give people a choice maybe between different parameters or recommendation logics.
best_quote.speaker: Natali Helberger

theme_codings.summary: This is the episode's dominant theme. Helberger argues transparency alone is insufficient without user agency, documents that 60% of Dutch respondents feel misled by undisclosed AI use, warns of label blindness, and highlights the alarming trust parity between news media and social media in using AI responsibly.

theme_codings.theme_name: The Ethics Infrastructure: From Principles to Practice

best_quote.text: Responsible use of AI is not simply something that you can write up in a guideline and that it will automatically materialize, but one also really needs to think of how that should be then internalized in working practices, in skills, in new routines.
best_quote.speaker: Natali Helberger

theme_codings.summary: Helberger reveals that nearly 20% of newsrooms adopt non-use as their AI ethics strategy, that responsible procurement is the least popular approach, and that guidelines-to-practice gaps persist. She advocates procurement checklists and emphasizes convergence across existing guidelines on shared principles.

theme_codings.theme_name: Building the Machine: Technical Architecture and Engineering Choices

best_quote.text: AI and the implementation of AI in newsrooms is often talked about as a technical problem. But I think this survey also very much showed it's not only a technical problem, it's also an ethical and a cultural problem.
best_quote.speaker: Natali Helberger

theme_codings.summary: Technical architecture is mentioned only to note that AI implementation challenges are not primarily technical, and that explainability research exists but remains unactionable for end users.

theme_codings.theme_name: The Literacy Imperative: Upskilling, Adoption, and the Knowledge Gap

best_quote.text: AI and the implementation of AI in newsrooms is often talked about as a technical problem. But I think this survey also very much showed it's not only a technical problem, it's also an ethical and a cultural problem.
best_quote.speaker: Natali Helberger

theme_codings.summary: Adoption barriers are touched upon in the context of nearly 20% of newsrooms refusing to use AI as an ethical stance, but training and literacy are not the episode's focus.

theme_codings.theme_name: Organizing for AI: Leadership, Strategy, and Institutional Change

best_quote.text: Responsible use of AI is not simply something that you can write up in a guideline and that it will automatically materialize, but one also really needs to think of how that should be then internalized in working practices, in skills, in new routines.
best_quote.speaker: Natali Helberger

theme_codings.summary: Organizational change is referenced when Helberger argues that ethics guidelines must be translated into working practices, skills, routines, funding, and incentives, but specific organizational structures are not discussed.

theme_codings.theme_name: Power, Money, and the Platform: Big Tech and the News Industry

best_quote.text: I think we really need to think of how to distinguish the media and make sure that people know if I want trustworthy content, I go there because it is fact-checked, there's human oversight, someone has been testing the system, there was responsible procurement, they are transparent about it.
best_quote.speaker: Natali Helberger

theme_codings.summary: The episode addresses the power dynamics between news media and social media platforms, arguing newsrooms must differentiate themselves on trust. Helberger notes that ecological concerns and Big Tech's role were notably absent from newsroom survey responses despite their prominence in public discourse.

theme_codings.theme_name: Reimagining the News Product: Personalization, Formats, and Audience Experience

best_quote.text: Being transparent about what parameter you use is very nice and good, but it starts only being really meaningful if you also give people a choice maybe between different parameters or recommendation logics.
best_quote.speaker: Natali Helberger

theme_codings.summary: Helberger discusses diversity metrics for recommendation algorithms (fragmentation, activation, representation) and the Panopticum project exploring user agency in expressing real preferences, framing personalization as a domain where ethical design and audience autonomy intersect.

theme_codings.theme_name: Survival Economics: Business Models, Revenue, and the Market for AI Journalism

best_quote.text: I think we really need to think of how to distinguish the media and make sure that people know if I want trustworthy content, I go there because it is fact-checked, there's human oversight, someone has been testing the system, there was responsible procurement, they are transparent about it.
best_quote.speaker: Natali Helberger

theme_codings.summary: Resource constraints for smaller newsrooms are mentioned in the context of their inability to access legal departments or reach large AI providers for support, but business models are not a primary focus.

theme_codings.theme_name: Regulating the Revolution: Law, Policy, and Governance of AI

best_quote.text: Simply the fact that something has been made with AI does not necessarily say that this is bad content. So I think it's a good first step. But I also think it doesn't tell you enough. So I don't think it will solve the deep fake issue.
best_quote.speaker: Natali Helberger

theme_codings.summary: The EU AI Act is referenced as facing enforcement challenges. Helberger critiques 'made with AI' labeling as regulation of the willing and argues for provenance-based approaches over simple labels, connecting to Council of Europe expert group work.

theme_codings.theme_name: The Prototype Pipeline: From Experiment to Production

best_quote.text: AI and the implementation of AI in newsrooms is often talked about as a technical problem. But I think this survey also very much showed it's not only a technical problem, it's also an ethical and a cultural problem.
best_quote.speaker: Natali Helberger

theme_codings.summary: The episode does not substantively address prototyping or production pipelines; its focus is on the ethical and audience-facing dimensions of AI adoption rather than the operational journey from experiment to deployment.

theme_codings.theme_name: Data, Privacy, and the Boundaries of Responsible AI Use

best_quote.text: AI and the implementation of AI in newsrooms is often talked about as a technical problem. But I think this survey also very much showed it's not only a technical problem, it's also an ethical and a cultural problem.
best_quote.speaker: Natali Helberger

theme_codings.summary: Data privacy surfaces in the procurement checklist discussion (data quality, data security, training data use) and in the broader concern about liability being dumped on end users by AI providers' terms of service.
